_meta.source: BYKI Mobile V2 — lib/engine/rules/ (all 7 system analyzers)
_meta.description: Complete rule set from V2's 7-system health scoring engine. Each rule contributes a weighted penalty to its system's health score. Health score = 100 − Σ(matched rule weight × 100). Rules are additive within systems.
_meta.language: Dart 3.12+ (transcribed to JSON for V5 use)

rule_structure.name: Descriptive name
rule_structure.description: Technical explanation of what this rule detects
rule_structure.thresholds: Numeric values used in the rule

special_notes.tropicalClimateOffset: Cooling system rules have +5°C on all temperature thresholds for Malaysian tropical climate
special_notes.oilRules: Oil system has NO direct oil quality PID — all rules are behavioral inference from other PIDs
special_notes.transmissionRules: Transmission rules use TCM-specific DIDs (Mode 22/23) not standard OBD Mode 01 — requires Rust agent

charging.description: Monitors alternator, battery, and charging circuit health
charging.healthScoring: 100 minus sum of triggered rule weights × 100

rules.name: Low Running Voltage
rules.description: Battery voltage below normal range while engine is running — charging system not maintaining proper voltage
possibleCauses: Weak battery failing to hold charge
rules.physicsNote: A healthy charging system maintains 13.5–14.8V while running. Below 12.9V indicates the alternator is not providing sufficient current above battery open-circuit voltage.

rules.name: Low Resting Voltage
rules.description: Battery voltage below 12.3V at idle — indicates discharged or failing battery
rules.physicsNote: Resting open-circuit voltage: 12.6V = 100% charge, 12.4V = 75%, 12.2V = 50%, 12.0V = 25%. Below 12.3V at idle is concerning.

rules.name: Low Charging Voltage
rules.description: Voltage at cruise RPM below 13.5V — alternator not meeting demand
rules.physicsNote: At cruise RPM, alternator output should comfortably exceed battery terminal voltage, providing 13.5–14.8V. Consistently below 13.5V at 1500+ RPM indicates alternator capacity issue.

rules.name: Overcharging
rules.description: System voltage exceeding 14.8V — voltage regulator failure risk of battery damage
possibleCauses: Voltage regulator stuck at full field
rules.physicsNote: Above 14.8V risks electrolyte boil-off in flooded batteries, accelerated grid corrosion. AGM batteries tolerant to ~14.9V. Above 15V risks electrical component damage.

rules.name: Flat Voltage vs RPM
rules.description: Voltage not increasing from idle to cruise — indicates flat alternator curve (possibly diode failure)
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage at RPM 2000 approximately equals voltage at RPM 800 (variance < 0.3V)
rules.physicsNote: A healthy alternator produces incrementally higher voltage as rotor speed increases (field current amplification). Flat response indicates rotor, stator, or rectifier issue.

rules.name: Voltage Fluctuation
rules.description: Voltage oscillating by more than 0.5V repeatedly — indicates intermittent contact or failing regulator
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage oscillates > 0.5V within short time window at stable RPM
rules.physicsNote: Voltage ripple is normal at <0.2V (AC component from rectified 3-phase). Visible fluctuations >0.5V indicate intermittent connection or regulator hunting.

rules.name: Voltage Drop Under Load
rules.description: Voltage drops below 12.5V when electrical loads activated — alternator cannot sustain demand
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage < 12.5V during high electrical load events (measured indirectly via load PID correlation)
possibleCauses: Battery unable to assist during peaks
rules.physicsNote: Alternator current output is RPM-dependent. At low RPM with high electrical demand (AC, headlights, heated seats), battery must supplement alternator. Voltage drop indicates insufficient capacity.

rules.name: Voltage Ripple at Idle
rules.description: Excessive voltage oscillation at idle RPM — indicates alternator diode failure or brush wear
rules.physicsNote: A failing diode causes AC ripple on the DC output. At idle (low RPM), capacitor filtering is weakest and ripple is most visible as voltage oscillation.

rules.name: Severe Overcharging
rules.description: System voltage exceeding 16V — voltage regulator has failed completely, risk of electrical damage
rules.physicsNote: Above 16V, electrolyte decomposes rapidly (hydrogen gas risk), ECU and sensor damage is likely. This is an emergency condition.

rules.name: Low Voltage at High RPM
rules.description: Voltage still below 13V despite high RPM — alternator output has failed
rules.physicsNote: At 2500+ RPM, even a weak alternator should produce 13V+. Below this indicates near-total alternator failure — battery is the sole power source.

rules.name: Idle/Cruise Voltage Identical
rules.description: No voltage difference between idle and cruise — alternator output not scaling with RPM
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage at idle ≈ voltage at cruise (diff < 0.1V) AND voltage < 14V
rules.physicsNote: Healthy alternators show 0.2-0.5V increase from idle to cruise. Flat response with low voltage means output is limited regardless of RPM.

rules.name: Heat-Related Voltage Drop
rules.description: Voltage drops when engine is hot — alternator performance degrades with underhood temperature
rules.physicsNote: Alternator output decreases with temperature — regulator compensates, but aged regulators lose compensation. Common in tropical underhood environment.

rules.name: Slow Charging Recovery
rules.description: Voltage range is very narrow despite RPM variation — alternator not responding to changing demand
possibleCauses: Alternator output saturated at low level
rules.physicsNote: A responsive charging system shows voltage variation as loads change. Narrow range at low voltage means the alternator is maxed out at insufficient capacity.

rules.name: High Airflow Low Voltage
rules.description: High MAF rate indicates high engine output, increasing alternator mechanical load — but voltage is below expected
rules.physicsNote: High MAF means high engine torque output. The alternator must absorb mechanical power to generate electricity. If belt slips under this combined load, voltage drops.

rules.name: Altitude Voltage Deficit
rules.description: At altitude (low baro), engine produces less power and alternator cooling is reduced — voltage may suffer
possibleCauses: Alternator de-rated at altitude due to reduced cooling airflow
Engine power loss reducing alternator RPM
rules.physicsNote: At 1500m altitude, air density drops ~15%. Alternator cooling is convection-based, so thermal derating occurs. Combined with lower engine power, the charging margin shrinks.

rules.name: Idle Voltage Sag Under Electrical Load
rules.description: Voltage drops significantly at idle when electrical loads are high — alternator RPM too low for demand
rules.triggerConditions: Voltage at idle < 13.0V AND idle RPM std dev > 50
rules.physicsNote: At idle (~700 RPM), alternator spins at 2100 RPM (3:1 ratio). Many alternators only produce 60-70% rated output at idle speed. High electrical loads can overwhelm this.

combustion.description: Monitors ignition quality, fuel combustion completion, O2 sensor activity, and misfire events

rules.name: LTFT Deviation
rules.description: Long-term fuel trim persistently positive or negative beyond ±10% — indicates chronic mixture offset
possibleCauses: Lean: Vacuum leak, low fuel pressure, clogged injector, MAF under-reading
Rich: Leaking injector, EVAP purge fault, MAP sensor offset
rules.physicsNote: LTFT is a learned offset stored in ECU memory. It compensates for systematic fueling errors by shifting base fuel map. Persistent deviation indicates a base calibration error the ECU is compensating for.

rules.name: STFT Instability
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim exhibiting large variance — indicates unstable mixture supply
rules.triggerConditions: STFT variance > 8% span in recent history
possibleCauses: Dirty throttle body causing mixture oscillation
rules.physicsNote: STFT is the real-time closed-loop correction. High variance indicates the ECU is making large, rapid corrections suggesting an unstable input (O2 signal or fuel supply).

rules.name: STFT Spike
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim spiking beyond ±15% momentarily — indicates acute fueling event
rules.physicsNote: ECU trim authority is typically ±25%. Spikes to ±15% indicate the ECU is near its correction limit, briefly. Fuel delivery drastically deviated from commanded.

rules.name: Active Misfire Detected
rules.description: OBD misfire counter above zero — unburned fuel being pushed into exhaust
possibleCauses: Compression loss (valve, piston ring, head gasket)
Ignition timing extreme out of range
rules.physicsNote: OBD misfire monitoring uses crank acceleration pattern. Each correctly fired cylinder produces measurable crankshaft acceleration pulse. Missing pulses = misfire. Each misfire pushes raw hydrocarbons into catalyst, risking thermal damage.

rules.name: Idle RPM Instability
rules.description: RPM at idle exhibiting oscillation — hunting idle indicates unstable combustion or idle control issue
rules.triggerConditions: RPM variance > 100 RPM at closed-throttle idle
rules.physicsNote: Idle stability is maintained by IAC (bypass air) and fuel trim cooperation. Hunting indicates a perturbation input (air leak or variable vacuum) that the idle control cannot dampen quickly enough.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Stuck Lean
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor voltage persistently below 0.2V for > 10 seconds — sensor contaminated or stuck
rules.triggerConditions: O2 voltage < 0.2V sustained AND closed loop
possibleCauses: O2 sensor contaminated with silicone or coolant
rules.physicsNote: A narrowband O2 sensor should switch between ~0.1V (lean) and ~0.9V (rich) in closed loop. Stuck low indicates either real severe lean condition or sensor failure preventing ECU from seeing rich excursion.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Stuck Rich
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor voltage persistently above 0.8V — ECU commanded rich or sensor stuck
rules.triggerConditions: O2 voltage > 0.8V sustained AND closed loop
possibleCauses: Large MAP/MAF sensor error causing over-fueling
rules.physicsNote: Sustained rich reading causes ECU to pull fuel trim negative. If trim hits -25% limit and mixture is still rich, indicates a fuel delivery override or sensor malfunction.

rules.name: O2 Low Variance (Lazy Sensor)
rules.description: Upstream O2 not switching actively — voltage variance below threshold indicating lazy/degraded sensor
rules.triggerConditions: O2 voltage variance < 0.3V over 30-second window in closed loop
possibleCauses: Aged O2 sensor with slow element response
O2 heater circuit failure causing cold sensor
rules.physicsNote: Healthy O2 sensors switch 3-8 times per second in closed loop at idle. Low variance indicates sensor is not cycling, meaning ECU cannot properly read lean/rich transitions needed for fuel control.

rules.name: Closed Loop Failure
rules.description: Engine not entering closed-loop fuel control after warmup — O2 heater or ECU mode fault
rules.triggerConditions: ECT > 70°C AND fuel_system_status indicates open loop
possibleCauses: O2 sensor not reaching operating temperature
ECU stuck in open-loop due to fault code
Coolant temperature sensor reading incorrectly (cold bias)
rules.physicsNote: ECU should transition to closed loop within 30-60 seconds of start once O2 sensor is warm. Remaining in open loop means ECU runs on default fuel maps, losing 5-15% fuel economy and increasing emissions.

rules.name: O2 Switching Frequency Low
rules.description: O2 upstream sensor crossing stoichiometry too infrequently — lazy/aged sensor
rules.triggerConditions: O2 crossing count < 3 over scan duration in closed loop
possibleCauses: Aged O2 sensor with degraded zirconia element
Sensor contaminated by silicone or coolant
Fuel mixture bias preventing normal oscillation
rules.physicsNote: A healthy NBO2 sensor crosses 0.45V multiple times per second in closed loop. Fewer than 1 crossing per 10 seconds of scan indicates a lazy sensor that cannot track lean/rich transitions fast enough for proper fuel control.

rules.name: Misfire Under Load
rules.description: Misfires occurring specifically under high load — ignition breakdown under compression
rules.physicsNote: Higher cylinder pressure under load requires more spark energy. Load-dependent misfires point to marginal ignition capacity failing under stress.

rules.name: Misfire at Idle
rules.description: Misfires detected at idle RPM — combustion instability at low speed
rules.physicsNote: Idle misfires cause rough running and are often felt as vibration. At idle, mixture sensitivity is highest — even small air/fuel imbalances cause combustion failure.

rules.name: Multi-Cylinder Misfire
rules.description: Two or more cylinders misfiring simultaneously — systemic combustion failure
rules.physicsNote: Multi-cylinder misfire = systemic cause. Individual cylinder misfires are local (plug/coil/injector), but simultaneous multi-cylinder failure indicates shared system fault.

rules.name: Excessive Timing Retard
rules.description: Timing advance below 5° at RPM > 2000 — heavy knock retard active
rules.physicsNote: Normal timing at 2000 RPM is 15-25° BTDC. Below 5° means severe knock retard — significant power loss (15-25%) and increased exhaust temperature.

rules.name: Negative Timing Advance
rules.description: Timing after TDC — extreme knock protection, near-zero power condition
rules.physicsNote: Negative timing = spark fires after piston starts descent. Almost zero useful work. Engine runs but produces minimal power. This is emergency knock protection.

rules.name: Over-Advanced Timing at Idle
rules.description: Timing advance above 25° at idle — unusual, may indicate low compression compensation
rules.physicsNote: Normal idle timing is 8-15° BTDC. Over 25° suggests the ECU is compensating for something — usually low cylinder pressure requiring more advance to develop adequate torque.

rules.name: Load/Throttle Correlation Low
rules.description: Engine load doesn't track throttle position — air system issue
rules.physicsNote: Load and throttle should be positively correlated. Low correlation means load is influenced by something other than throttle — unmetered air, EGR flow, or sensor error.

rules.name: RPM Instability While Driving
rules.description: RPM standard deviation excessive while vehicle is moving — engine hunting or transmission issues
rules.triggerConditions: RPM std dev > 200 AND avg speed > 30 km/h
rules.physicsNote: At cruise, RPM should be very stable (±50 RPM). Variation > 200 RPM while driving indicates either engine-side instability or drivetrain engagement issues.

rules.name: O2 Bank Imbalance
rules.description: Bank 1 and Bank 2 upstream O2 readings significantly different — bank-specific mixture issue
rules.physicsNote: On V-engines, each bank has independent fueling. Imbalance > 0.2V average means one bank is running significantly different mixture than the other.

rules.name: Catalyst Efficiency Degraded
rules.description: Catalyst efficiency ratio between 0.5-0.7 — catalyst aging but not yet failed
rules.physicsNote: Catalyst efficiency decreases as precious metal coating degrades. Ratio 0.5-0.7 is the warning zone — catalyst is still functional but approaching failure threshold.

rules.name: MAP vs Timing Mismatch
rules.description: High MAP (high load) should have retarded timing for knock protection — if timing is still advanced, knock control may be faulty
rules.triggerConditions: MAP > 80 kPa AND timing > 25° AND RPM > 2000
rules.physicsNote: At high MAP (near atmospheric = high load), cylinder pressure rises, increasing knock tendency. Normal timing retard to 10-15° is expected. >25° at high MAP risks detonation.

rules.name: Volumetric Efficiency Below Normal
rules.description: MAF vs RPM ratio indicates VE significantly below normal — engine breathing restricted
rules.triggerConditions: MAF / RPM ratio < 0.003 g/s per RPM at WOT
rules.physicsNote: Typical 2.0L NA engine at 3000 RPM WOT should flow ~18 g/s MAF. Ratio < 0.003 on a normal displacement engine suggests 20-30% VE loss.

rules.name: Misfires Under High Load
rules.description: Misfires detected when load is high — more damaging than idle misfires due to higher cylinder pressure
rules.physicsNote: High load = high cylinder pressure = harder to ignite. Marginal ignition components that work at idle fail under load. Unburned fuel damages catalysts.

rules.name: O2 Upstream Frozen (No Crossings)
rules.description: Upstream O2 not switching between rich/lean — sensor may be stuck or heater failed
rules.triggerConditions: O2 upstream crossings < 3 per 10 seconds in closed loop
rules.physicsNote: A healthy narrowband O2 switches 1-3 Hz. Fewer than 3 crossings in 10s means the sensor is lazy or stuck — ECU fuel control precision degrades severely.

rules.name: Excessive Timing Advance When Cold
rules.description: Timing is excessively advanced during cold start — should be retarded for catalyst light-off
possibleCauses: ECT sensor reading higher than actual
rules.physicsNote: Cold start timing should be retarded 5-15° to increase exhaust heat for catalyst light-off. Advanced timing during cold start reduces exhaust temperature and slows catalyst activation.

rules.name: Average Timing Below Expected
rules.description: Average timing advance persistently low across driving — may indicate chronic knock or mechanical retard
rules.physicsNote: Most engines run 15-25° average timing. Persistent sub-8° suggests the ECU is constantly retarding — either from knock or because base timing is mechanically off.

fuel.description: Monitors fuel delivery system: injectors, fuel pump, pressure regulation, and mixture formation

rules.name: Persistent Lean LTFT
rules.description: LTFT persistently positive beyond +10% — ECU learned that base fueling is lean
possibleCauses: Low fuel pressure reducing injection quantity
rules.physicsNote: LTFT positive = ECU adding fuel on top of base map because mixture runs lean. Consistent lean LTFT indicates a systematic un-metered air or under-fuel condition.

rules.name: Persistent Rich LTFT
rules.description: LTFT persistently negative beyond -10% — ECU learned base fueling is rich
possibleCauses: Leaking fuel injectors adding excess fuel
Saturated EVAP canister purging excess HC
MAF sensor over-reading causing excess fueling
rules.physicsNote: LTFT negative = ECU reducing fuel to compensate for systematic over-rich condition. Common after EVAP canister saturation or injector o-ring leaks.

rules.name: STFT Spikes
rules.description: STFT exhibiting sudden spikes beyond ±20% — acute fuel delivery event
rules.physicsNote: ECU trim limits are typically ±25-30%. Spikes near limit indicate the ECU is at maximum correction. If air/fuel ratio still wrong at trim limits, misfire or DTC is imminent.

rules.name: Lean Under Load
rules.description: Positive fuel trim increasing specifically under high load — fuel system cannot meet demand
possibleCauses: Weak fuel pump failing under demand
Pressure regulator failing under high flow
rules.physicsNote: Fuel pump must maintain pressure under peak flow demands. Restriction causes pressure drop under high injection pulse widths needed for WOT.

rules.name: Lean Persistence
rules.description: LTFT trending lean over multiple drive cycles — progressive lean condition developing
rules.triggerConditions: LTFT trending positive across sessions above 8%
rules.physicsNote: LTFT is session-persistent (stored in KAM). Progressive increase across sessions indicates a worsening fuel delivery or air metering problem.

rules.name: Rich Persistence
rules.description: LTFT trending rich over multiple drive cycles — progressive over-fueling condition
rules.triggerConditions: LTFT trending negative across sessions below -8%
rules.physicsNote: Negative LTFT trending progressively indicates worsening over-fueling. Often caused by injector o-ring degradation or cracked fuel pressure regulator diaphragm.

rules.name: O2 and Trim Correlation Lean
rules.description: High positive LTFT combined with O2 readings confirming lean mixture — multi-signal lean confirmation
possibleCauses: Low fuel pressure with MAF reading correctly
rules.physicsNote: When both LTFT and O2 signal confirm lean, this eliminates sensor disagreement and confirms actual lean mixture in cylinder.

rules.name: Bank 2 Lean Deviation
rules.description: Bank 2 LTFT significantly leaner than Bank 1 — bank-specific fuel delivery issue
possibleCauses: Intake manifold gasket leak on B2 side
Exhaust manifold crack leaking air before O2 sensor
VVT phaser issue on B2 bank
rules.physicsNote: Bank differential above 5-7% is a strong indicator of a bank-specific air or fuel issue, rather than a global fuel delivery problem.

rules.name: Idle vs Load Trim Mismatch
rules.description: Fuel trims differ significantly between idle and load conditions — indicates context-specific fuel issue
possibleCauses: Vacuum leak (lean at idle, normal at load when vacuum drops)
Fuel pressure regulator fault (pressure changes with load)
MAF sensor contamination (reads differently at different flow rates)
EGR stuck open (affects idle more than load)
rules.physicsNote: A vacuum leak causes lean condition primarily at idle when manifold vacuum is highest. As load increases and vacuum drops, the leak's proportional effect diminishes. A >5% trim difference between idle and load is a classic vacuum leak signature.

rules.name: Total Fuel Trim Excessive Lean
rules.description: Combined STFT + LTFT exceeding +20% — both short and long term corrections pulling lean
rules.physicsNote: Combined trim > 20% means the ECU has learned a lean base (LTFT) AND is still adding fuel (STFT). The problem exceeds learned compensation — it's worsening.

rules.name: Total Fuel Trim Excessive Rich
rules.description: Combined STFT + LTFT below -20% — both corrections pulling fuel (rich)
rules.physicsNote: Combined negative trim > 20% means systematic over-fueling that learned correction can't fully compensate. Catalytic converter overload risk.

rules.name: Fuel Rail Pressure Low
rules.description: Direct fuel rail pressure measurement below normal operating range
rules.physicsNote: Port injection: typical 270-310 kPa. Below 250 kPa means injectors can't deliver rated flow at full pulse width — lean under load.

rules.name: Fuel Rail Pressure High
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure above normal range — regulator stuck or return line blocked
rules.physicsNote: High pressure causes rich condition — each injector pulse delivers more fuel than calibrated. Negative LTFT is the expected compensation.

rules.name: Open Loop While Warm and Driving
rules.description: Fuel system not in closed loop despite warm engine and driving conditions — sensor fault
rules.triggerConditions: Not closed loop AND ECT > 80°C AND speed > 30 km/h
rules.physicsNote: After warmup, closed loop is mandatory for emissions and efficiency. Open loop at cruise = 10-15% fuel economy penalty and higher emissions.

rules.name: Trims Pulling Same Direction
rules.description: Both STFT and LTFT positive or both negative — condition worsening beyond learned correction
rules.physicsNote: LTFT is the learned base. STFT on top in the same direction means the learned value isn't enough — the problem is getting worse.

rules.name: Bank-to-Bank Trim Asymmetry
rules.description: Bank 1 and Bank 2 LTFT differ by more than 8% — bank-specific fuel delivery issue
rules.physicsNote: Both banks share fuel pressure — asymmetric trims isolate the problem to one bank. Useful for V-engine cylinder-bank isolation.

rules.name: Critical Fuel Level
rules.description: Fuel level below 8% — risk of fuel pump overheating and air ingestion
rules.physicsNote: Fuel pump is cooled by immersion in fuel. Below 8%, pump may be exposed to air — accelerates pump motor wear and risks air ingestion into fuel lines.

rules.name: Low Pressure + Lean Trims
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure low AND LTFT lean — confirms fuel delivery failure
rules.physicsNote: Low pressure + lean trims is a definitive fuel delivery failure pattern. The ECU is compensating (lean trims) because physical fuel delivery is insufficient.

rules.name: MAF vs Fuel Pressure Demand Gap
rules.description: High MAF (high demand) but fuel pressure dropping — pump can't keep up with flow demand
rules.triggerConditions: MAF > 20 g/s AND fuel pressure < 280 kPa
possibleCauses: Fuel filter restriction at high flow
rules.physicsNote: At 20+ g/s MAF, injector pulse width is near maximum. Fuel pressure must be maintained for proper atomization. Pressure drop here causes lean misfire at WOT.

rules.name: STFT High Variance at Idle
rules.description: STFT rapid oscillation at idle indicates intermittent lean/rich swings — unstable fuel control
rules.physicsNote: Normal STFT variance is < 5% at stable idle. High variance means the ECU is constantly chasing the mixture — either a real disturbance or a slow O2 sensor creating overshoot.

rules.name: Fuel Level Sender Stuck
rules.description: Fuel level unchanged over extended runtime — sender unit likely stuck
rules.triggerConditions: Fuel level unchanged AND runtime > 1800s AND distance > 20 km
rules.physicsNote: At highway driving, typical fuel consumption is 6-10 L/100km. After 20 km, fuel level should have dropped at least 1-2 liters. No change = sender malfunction.

rules.name: Altitude Fuel Correction Check
rules.description: At altitude (low baro), LTFT should compensate for thinner air if MAF-based — checks for altitude adaptation
possibleCauses: Speed-density system not compensating for altitude
rules.physicsNote: At 1500m altitude, air is ~15% less dense. MAF-based systems auto-compensate. Speed-density systems must use baro correction. Excess LTFT at altitude = poor baro compensation.

rules.name: High MAP with Lean STFT (Vacuum Leak Signature)
rules.description: Elevated MAP pressure at idle combined with positive STFT — classic unmetered air leak pattern
rules.triggerConditions: MAP > 55 kPa at idle AND STFT > 8%
rules.physicsNote: Normal idle MAP is 25-45 kPa. Unmetered air entering after MAF/throttle body raises MAP and causes lean (positive STFT). Both moving together is the definitive vacuum leak fingerprint.

rules.name: Persistent Fuel Trim Bank Imbalance
rules.description: O2 bank imbalance sustained across session — one bank consistently different from the other
possibleCauses: Injector on one bank partially clogged
Exhaust manifold crack on one bank
Intake manifold runner leak on one bank
rules.physicsNote: V-engine banks should run within 2% of each other for fuel trim. Sustained imbalance indicates a bank-specific issue — not a common system fault.

cooling.description: Monitors thermostat, coolant circulation, radiator, and thermal management efficiency. All temp thresholds include +5°C offset for Malaysian tropical climate.

rules.name: Slow Warm-Up
rules.description: Engine not reaching at least 75°C within 10 minutes of start — thermostat stuck open
rules.physicsNote: A thermostat should start opening at ~88°C and be fully open by ~102°C. Engine that doesn't reach 75°C after 10 minutes has severely impaired thermal management — will run over-rich and reduce fuel economy.

rules.name: Low Warm-Up Rate
rules.description: Engine temp rising too slowly even before thermostat opens — indicates low coolant or water pump issue
rules.triggerConditions: ECT rise rate < 0.1°C/sec during initial warm-up
rules.physicsNote: Normal pre-thermostat warm-up rate is 0.2-0.5°C per second depending on ambient temp and idle load. Below 0.1°C/s indicates the coolant mass is absorbing heat very slowly — possibly large air pocket or low level.

rules.name: Overheating
rules.description: Coolant temperature above 110°C — engine in danger zone
rules.physicsNote: Above 105°C risks coolant boiling (if cap failed), aluminum alloy annealing, head gasket distortion. At 115°C, ECU should trigger cooling measures or shutdown protection.

rules.name: Elevated Temperature
rules.description: Temperature in warning zone 100-110°C — not yet critical but heating beyond design point
possibleCauses: Towing / heavy load on hot day
rules.physicsNote: The elevated range indicates cooling system is marginal. Under normal conditions it recovers, but sustained operation risks progressive degradation.

rules.name: Temperature Fluctuation
rules.description: Coolant temperature oscillating >10°C repeatedly at steady state — thermostat or circulation issue
possibleCauses: Coolant level low causing intermittent circulation
Water pump impeller slipping on shaft
rules.physicsNote: Normal thermostat operation causes ±3-5°C oscillation. Fluctuations above 10°C indicate the thermostat is opening/closing too aggressively or coolant flow is intermittent.

rules.name: Suboptimal Operating Temperature
rules.description: Engine running below optimal 82-95°C window even when warm — efficiency impact
possibleCauses: Cold ambient temperature with heavy air flow
rules.physicsNote: Optimal combustion efficiency occurs at 90-95°C where fuel atomization, oil viscosity, and catalytic converter efficiency are all optimal. Below 82°C engine runs enriched, increasing fuel consumption and wear.

rules.name: Cruise Temperature Drop
rules.description: Temperature dropping significantly when speed increases — thermostat stuck open allowing excessive coolant flow
rules.triggerConditions: ECT drops > 15°C when speed increases from idle to cruise
possibleCauses: Cooling fan running continuously at high speed
rules.physicsNote: With thermostat stuck open, increased coolant flow at higher pump speed (RPM/speed) allows radiator to over-cool the coolant. Temperature should be stable across speed ranges with a functioning thermostat.

rules.name: ECT Sensor Implausibly High
rules.description: Coolant temp reading > 130°C — likely sensor short circuit rather than actual temperature
rules.physicsNote: NTC thermistor short = low resistance = reads very hot. Actual coolant rarely exceeds 120°C even in failure. Above 130°C is almost certainly sensor fault.

rules.name: ECT Sensor Implausibly Low
rules.description: Coolant temp < 20°C after 10 minutes — sensor open circuit causing false cold reading
rules.physicsNote: In tropical climate, ambient is 25-38°C. ECT below 20°C after 10 min is physically impossible — sensor has open-circuit reading maximum cold. Causes perpetual cold enrichment.

rules.name: Cooling Fan Not Engaging
rules.description: ECT above fan activation point while moving slowly — fan not handling heat load
rules.physicsNote: Fan should activate at 95-102°C. At low speed, no ram airflow means fan is sole cooling. ECT > 100°C at low speed means fan is inadequate or not running.

rules.name: Slow Warmup Despite Hot Ambient
rules.description: Warmup rate too slow when intake air temp is high — should warm faster in hot ambient
rules.physicsNote: Higher ambient temperature should accelerate engine warmup. Slow warmup in hot conditions strongly suggests thermostat not closing to retain combustion heat.

rules.name: Thermal Soak at Idle
rules.description: ECT above 98°C while idling stationary — heat building without dissipation
rules.triggerConditions: ECT > 98°C AND RPM < 900 AND speed < 5 km/h
rules.physicsNote: At idle, no ram air and low coolant pump speed. Tropical ambient (30-38°C) further reduces ΔT. Some rise is expected, but sustained > 98°C suggests marginal cooling.

rules.name: Head Gasket Suspect Pattern
rules.description: Overheating combined with lean trims — possible combustion gas in cooling system
rules.physicsNote: Combustion pressure enters cooling system (overheating) while coolant enters cylinder (lean). This dual symptom pattern is highly indicative of head gasket failure.

rules.name: IAT Exceeds ECT When Warm
rules.description: Intake air temp higher than coolant temp when engine warm — sensor fault likely
rules.physicsNote: Coolant should always be hotter than intake air once warm. IAT > ECT suggests one sensor is reading incorrectly.

rules.name: Extremely Slow Warmup Rate
rules.description: Warmup rate below 0.5°C per minute — thermostat likely stuck open allowing continuous coolant flow
rules.physicsNote: Normal warmup rate is 1-3°C/min. Below 0.5°C/min means coolant is continuously circulating through radiator during warmup — thermostat should be blocking this flow until 80-90°C.

rules.name: Coolant Temperature Oscillation
rules.description: Coolant temp fluctuating more than expected at operating temp — thermostat chattering or air pocket
rules.triggerConditions: Coolant temp std dev > 4°C when at operating temp
rules.physicsNote: Normal coolant temp std dev is < 2°C at steady state. Oscillation > 4°C indicates the thermostat is hunting between open and closed, often due to a weak wax element or air pocket.

rules.name: Temperature Rising During Highway Cruise
rules.description: Coolant temp rising despite highway speed airflow — cooling capacity insufficient for heat rejection
rules.triggerConditions: Cruise temp trend > 0.5°C/min AND speed > 60 km/h
possibleCauses: Head gasket allowing combustion gas into coolant
rules.physicsNote: At 60+ km/h, ram air provides maximum radiator cooling. If temp rises under these conditions, the cooling system has a fundamental capacity deficit — not an airflow problem.

rules.name: Maximum Coolant Temperature Danger Zone
rules.description: Maximum recorded coolant temp exceeds 115°C — engine damage threshold
rules.physicsNote: Above 115°C, aluminum heads warp, head gaskets fail, and oil viscosity collapses. Factory red-zone is typically 110-120°C. This is immediate stop-driving territory.

rules.name: Low Cooling Margin in Hot Ambient
rules.description: ECT close to overheating threshold in hot ambient conditions — reduced cooling margin
rules.triggerConditions: ECT > 100°C AND ambient estimate > 35°C AND speed < 30 km/h
possibleCauses: Coolant concentration too high (reduced heat transfer)
rules.physicsNote: In Malaysian tropical climate (35-40°C), the coolant-to-air ΔT is reduced from 80°C to 65°C — cutting radiator efficiency by ~20%. Fan and airflow become critical.

emission.description: Monitors catalytic converter efficiency, O2 sensor pair activity, and EGR function

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Mirroring Pre-Cat
rules.description: Downstream O2 switching at similar frequency to upstream — catalyst not storing oxygen
rules.triggerConditions: Downstream O2 switching rate > 70% of upstream rate
rules.physicsNote: A healthy catalyst uses its cerium oxide oxygen storage to buffer lean/rich spikes. Post-cat O2 should see this buffered, stable exhaust. If it mirrors upstream, storage capacity is exhausted — catalyst is depleted.

rules.name: Reduced O2 Storage Capacity
rules.description: Post-cat O2 responding too quickly to upstream changes — catalyst storage depleted
rules.triggerConditions: Time lag between upstream and downstream O2 response < 2 seconds
rules.physicsNote: Oxygen storage capacity (OSC) is the key catalyst performance metric. Healthy catalyst has 0.5-2g oxygen storage. Depleted catalyst shows < 0.1g — measured indirectly by response speed.

rules.name: Low Upstream-Downstream Voltage Difference
rules.description: Average voltage difference between upstream and downstream O2 too small
possibleCauses: Both O2 sensors reading similar values — catalyst transparent
Downstream sensor biased to upstream value
rules.physicsNote: Upstream sensor swings ±0.5V around 0.5V. Downstream held stable by catalyst should read ~0.55-0.65V. Small or reversed difference indicates catalyst not performing its storage function.

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Unstable
rules.description: Downstream O2 exhibiting large unexplained oscillation — catalyst degraded or exhaust leak
rules.triggerConditions: Downstream O2 variance > 0.2V at steady state
rules.physicsNote: Downstream O2 should be stable (±0.05V) except during deliberate rich/lean transitions for OSC testing. Instability indicates variable exhaust composition reaching the sensor.

rules.name: Downstream O2 Stuck High
rules.description: Post-cat O2 permanently high (>0.7V) — running rich through catalyst or sensor fault
possibleCauses: Engine running very rich through catalyst
rules.physicsNote: Post-cat persistent high voltage indicates sustained rich exhaust gas. Healthy catalyst should consume excess hydrocarbons, normalizing exhaust gas lambda.

rules.name: Downstream O2 Stuck Low
rules.description: Post-cat O2 permanently low (<0.3V) — sensor failure or exhaust leak before downstream sensor
possibleCauses: O2 sensor heater failure keeping element cold
Exhaust leak ahead of downstream sensor adding O2
rules.physicsNote: Post-cat sensor stuck lean is most often a failed heater circuit, preventing sensor element from reaching operating temperature (350°C minimum for zirconia cells).

rules.name: High LTFT with Degraded Catalyst
rules.description: Both lean LTFT and catalyst degradation signals present — combined emissions efficiency concern
rules.triggerConditions: LTFT > 10% AND downstream O2 mirroring upstream
possibleCauses: System running lean (high HC, low CO) AND catalyst unable to oxidize remaining HC
rules.physicsNote: Lean condition increases NOx emission even as HC decreases. Degraded converter no longer reduces NOx effectively. Combined — all three major pollutants (HC, CO, NOx) at risk.

rules.name: Lambda Upstream Deviation
rules.description: O2 upstream average not centering around stoichiometry (0.5V on NBO2 = Lambda 1.0)
rules.triggerConditions: O2 upstream 30s average outside 0.4-0.6V in closed loop
rules.physicsNote: In closed loop, ECU drives mixture toward stoichiometric Lambda=1.0 (AFR 14.7:1 gasoline). O2 should oscillate around 0.5V on a conventional NBO2. Bias indicates trim limits reached or sensor error.

rules.name: Recently Cleared DTCs
rules.description: DTCs recently cleared — monitors and readiness flags may be incomplete, possible underlying issue masked
possibleCauses: Codes cleared to pass inspection without repair
Battery disconnect reset codes but problem persists
rules.physicsNote: OBD-II readiness monitors require specific drive cycles to complete. Recently cleared codes mean monitors haven't run yet, so emissions compliance is indeterminate. Flag to user to complete a drive cycle before trusting health scores.

rules.name: Extended MIL Distance
rules.description: MIL (Check Engine Light) has been on for extended distance — potential catalyst or emission damage from unresolved fault
rules.physicsNote: Extended driving with MIL risks catalyst damage from unburned fuel, increased NOx from lean conditions, or oil contamination from persistent misfire. P0420/P0430 alone may be tolerable, but P030x misfires cause direct catalyst harm.

rules.name: Catalyst Temperature Critical
rules.description: Catalyst temperature above 850°C — substrate meltdown risk
rules.physicsNote: Catalyst normal range: 350-600°C. Above 800°C, substrate begins to sinter. Above 1000°C, complete meltdown blocks exhaust flow.

rules.name: Catalyst Not Reaching Temperature
rules.description: Catalyst below light-off temperature when engine is warm — reduced conversion efficiency
rules.triggerConditions: Cat temp < 300°C AND ECT > 80°C AND runtime > 300s
rules.physicsNote: Catalyst light-off ~250°C. Below 300°C when warm means insufficient exhaust enthalpy to maintain catalytic conversion. Emissions increase significantly.

rules.name: EGR Stuck Open at Idle
rules.description: EGR valve passing exhaust gas at idle — should be 0% at idle
rules.physicsNote: EGR at idle dilutes charge causing rough running, low power, and unstable idle. EGR is only beneficial at part-load cruise conditions.

rules.name: EGR Position Error Excessive
rules.description: Difference between commanded and actual EGR position exceeds 10%
rules.physicsNote: Modern EGR valves have position feedback. Error > 10% means the valve isn't going where told — carbon buildup is the most common cause.

rules.name: EVAP Purge Valve Stuck Open
rules.description: EVAP purge valve open at idle causing rich mixture from fuel vapors
rules.triggerConditions: EVAP purge > 50% AND RPM < 900 AND LTFT < -8%
rules.physicsNote: Purge should be 0% at idle. Stuck open floods engine with fuel vapors — equivalent to a rich fuel leak. ECU compensates with negative LTFT.

rules.name: EVAP System Not Purging
rules.description: EVAP purge never activating when conditions allow — canister filling with vapors
rules.triggerConditions: EVAP purge < 1% AND load > 30% AND ECT > 70°C
rules.physicsNote: EVAP purge is emissions-mandatory. Failed purge means fuel vapors vent to atmosphere instead of being burned in engine.

rules.name: Rich Running + Catalyst Heat
rules.description: Rich fuel trims combined with elevated catalyst temperature — catalyst converting excess fuel to heat
rules.physicsNote: Catalyst oxidizes excess HC — more fuel = more exothermic heat. Sustained rich running above 650°C accelerates precious metal sintering.

rules.name: Open Loop at Cruise (Emission Impact)
rules.description: Not in closed loop while driving warm — emissions uncontrolled
rules.triggerConditions: Not closed loop AND speed > 40 km/h AND ECT > 70°C
rules.physicsNote: Closed loop O2 feedback is the primary emission control strategy. Without it, fuel mixture is open-map — typically 5-15% richer than optimal for emissions.

rules.name: EGR Active at Idle (Should Be Off)
rules.description: EGR commanding flow at idle when it should be zero — causes rough idle and high HC
rules.triggerConditions: Commanded EGR at idle > 3% AND RPM < 900
possibleCauses: Carbon preventing EGR valve from seating
rules.physicsNote: EGR dilutes intake charge. At idle, there's barely enough charge for stable combustion. Even 3% EGR at idle causes noticeable roughness and increased HC emissions.

rules.name: Persistent EGR Position Error
rules.description: Average EGR error sustained above normal — valve not reaching commanded position
rules.physicsNote: EGR position accuracy of ±2% is needed for proper NOx control. Above 5% average error, the actual EGR flow deviates enough to affect NOx/HC balance.

rules.name: Catalyst Overtemperature
rules.description: Catalyst temperature exceeds 800°C — substrate damage threshold for ceramic catalysts
possibleCauses: Extended rich running (excess fuel burning in cat)
Misfire sending unburned fuel to cat
Engine running lean at WOT (excess heat)
rules.physicsNote: Ceramic catalyst substrate begins to melt/sinter above 850°C. At 800°C, precious metal coating agglomerates rapidly, permanently reducing active surface area and efficiency.

rules.name: Excessive EVAP Purge Rate
rules.description: EVAP purge rate very high — may overwhelm fuel control with vapor
rules.physicsNote: EVAP purge at >80% dumps large amounts of fuel vapor into intake. If charcoal canister is saturated (overfilled tank), vapor is too rich for STFT to compensate.

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Lean Drift
rules.description: Downstream O2 reading lean — catalyst no longer storing sufficient oxygen
rules.triggerConditions: O2 downstream < 0.3V sustained AND catalyst efficiency ratio > 0.6
rules.physicsNote: A healthy catalyst stores and releases oxygen to buffer AFR swings. When oxygen storage capacity is depleted, post-cat O2 mirrors pre-cat — reading lean during lean excursions.

rules.name: Extended Driving With MIL Active
rules.description: Vehicle has been driven significant distance with check engine light on — unresolved emission fault
rules.physicsNote: MIL indicates the emission system is operating outside legal limits. Extended driving increases catalyst damage risk if the underlying fault causes rich/lean excursions.

oil.description: Behavioral oil degradation inference — NO direct oil quality PID. Rules infer oil stress from thermal, speed, RPM, and usage patterns. Used to predict oil change urgency.
oil.note: All rules are behavioral inference. Oil health is estimated, not directly measured.

rules.name: Thermal Stress Accumulation
rules.description: Coolant temperature persistently high — oil experiencing thermal degradation above 110°C
possibleCauses: Cooling system dysfunction allowing oil to overheat
rules.physicsNote: Oil degradation rate doubles for every 10°C above normal operating range (Arrhenius rule). Above 120°C, base oil oxidation accelerates exponentially, reducing interfacial film strength and increasing viscosity breakdown.

rules.name: High RPM Operation Stress
rules.description: Sustained high RPM operation stressing oil film at bearing surfaces
possibleCauses: Spirited driving above oil design load
rules.physicsNote: At high RPM, hydrodynamic oil film at bearings must withstand increasing centrifugal and shear forces. High-shear viscosity stability (HTHS) is the key metric — oils meeting SAE J300 HTHS > 3.5 cP are preferred.

rules.name: Excessive Idle Accumulation
rules.description: High proportion of running time spent at idle — reduces oil film pressure and allows fuel/water dilution
rules.triggerConditions: Idle fraction > 40% of run time (RPM < 900 AND throttle < 5%)
rules.physicsNote: At idle, oil pump barely maintains minimum pressure. Coolant and oil temperatures may not fully burn off condensation and fuel blow-by, causing dilution. Short-trip/idle-heavy cycles are particularly hard on oil.

rules.name: Mileage-Based Stress Indicator
rules.description: Accumulated distance since inferred oil change — approaching service interval
rules.triggerConditions: Inferred distance since last service > 7000 km
rules.note: V5 should track session-cumulative distance if no external service record is available
rules.physicsNote: Conventional oil is typically rated for 5000-7500 km. Full synthetic 10000-15000 km. Base rule uses 7000 km conservative threshold for Malaysian tropical conditions.

rules.name: Short Trip Pattern
rules.description: Regular short trips preventing oil from reaching full operating temperature — causes accelerated contamination
rules.triggerConditions: ECT never exceeds 80°C within a session AND run time > 10 min
possibleCauses: Urban driving with stops before full warm-up
rules.physicsNote: Below ~85°C engine operating temperature, water and fuel vapors condense into oil rather than evaporating out. Short trip duty cycle is identified by industry as the most damaging to oil condition.

rules.name: Combined High Stress
rules.description: Multiple stress factors active simultaneously — accelerated oil degradation probability
rules.triggerConditions: Three or more of: heat stress, high RPM, long idle, long distance all triggers active concurrently
rules.physicsNote: Oil degradation is multiplicative, not additive under multiple stressors. High temp + high load + extended interval = oil life severely shortened beyond linear interpolation.

rules.name: Malaysian Tropical Idle Penalty
rules.description: Idle penalty adjusted for Malaysian climate — AC compressor load increases idle thermal stress beyond standard thresholds
rules.triggerConditions: Idle fraction > 30% in high ambient temperature environment
rules.note: Tropical climate (Malaysia-specific): AC compressor adds ~3-5 kW parasitic load at all times, raising idle ECT by 5-8°C vs temperate baseline
rules.physicsNote: In Malaysia, air conditioning is mandatory in virtually all vehicles. AC creates additional idle load that temperate-climate oil change intervals don't account for. This rule applies an offset reducing the idle threshold trigger.

rules.name: Direct Oil Temp High
rules.description: Direct oil temperature reading exceeding safe operating limit (when PID 0x015C available)
rules.physicsNote: Direct oil temperature measurement is available on modern vehicles via OBD-II PID 0x015C. Above 130°C is critical — API service limit for most engine oils. Immediate enrichment and thermal protection strategies recommended.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Low at Warm Idle
rules.description: Oil pressure critically low when engine is warm at idle — bearing protection compromised
rules.triggerConditions: Oil pressure < 70 kPa AND RPM < 900 AND ECT > 80°C
rules.physicsNote: Minimum safe idle pressure: 70-100 kPa. Below this, hydrodynamic oil film breaks down — metal-to-metal bearing contact begins.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Not Rising with RPM
rules.description: Oil pressure stays low even at elevated RPM — pump output insufficient or major internal leak
rules.physicsNote: At 2500+ RPM, oil pressure should be 200-400 kPa. Below 150 means pump can't build adequate pressure — end-stage engine wear.

rules.name: Oil Temperature Extreme
rules.description: Oil temperature above 140°C — immediate and severe oil degradation
rules.physicsNote: At 140°C, oil life is 1/16 of rated. Oil coking begins in tight passages (turbo bearings, piston ring grooves). Immediate engine shutdown recommended.

rules.name: Fuel Dilution Suspected
rules.description: Persistent rich condition plus elevated oil temp — fuel washing past rings into crankcase
rules.physicsNote: Fuel dilution reduces oil viscosity and load-carrying capacity. More than 3% fuel in oil requires immediate oil change.

rules.name: Pressure-per-RPM Ratio Low
rules.description: TSM oil pressure divided by RPM ratio is abnormally low — pump wear or bearing leak-back
rules.physicsNote: Oil pressure should scale roughly linearly with RPM (pump output). A low ratio means disproportionate pressure loss — either the pump is weak or the bearings leak too much.

rules.name: Compound Thermal Stress (ECT + Oil)
rules.description: Both coolant and oil temperatures elevated simultaneously — severe thermal event
possibleCauses: Oil cooler and radiator both restricted
rules.physicsNote: When both coolant and oil overheat, neither system can buffer the other. This compounds damage — oil degrades faster when coolant can't absorb heat, and vice versa.

rules.name: High RPM Before Warmup
rules.description: Engine revved above 4000 RPM while coolant still cold — oil not at operating viscosity
rules.physicsNote: Cold oil viscosity is 10-100x higher than warm. High RPM with cold oil causes excessive pump cavitation, inadequate film thickness at bearings, and accelerated cam/lifter wear.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Declining Over Session
rules.description: Oil pressure slowly declining during session at constant RPM — viscosity dropping as oil overheats or pump wearing
rules.triggerConditions: Oil pressure trend declining > 10 kPa AND oil temp > 110°C
rules.physicsNote: Oil viscosity halves approximately every 20°C above 100°C. At 120°C, a 5W-30 oil flows like water — film thickness at bearings becomes critically thin.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Unstable at Idle
rules.description: Oil pressure fluctuating at idle more than expected — oil level low or pump cavitating
rules.triggerConditions: Oil pressure variance high at idle AND RPM < 900
possibleCauses: Oil level between min and add marks
Oil pump pickup tube partially exposed
rules.physicsNote: At idle, oil pump output is at minimum. Any air ingestion (low level) causes pressure cycling. Coolant-contaminated oil foams, further reducing effective pump output.

rules.name: Oil Temp Rising, Pressure Falling
rules.description: Oil temperature increasing while pressure decreases — viscosity breakdown reducing pump effectiveness
rules.triggerConditions: Oil temp > 115°C AND oil pressure < 100 kPa at RPM > 1500
rules.physicsNote: Oil pressure at cruise should be 200-350 kPa. Below 100 kPa at 1500+ RPM means oil film is inadequate for rod and main bearings — imminent bearing damage risk.

rules.name: Excessive High-RPM Driving (Oil Stress)
rules.description: More than 30% of driving time above 4000 RPM — accelerates oil shearing and thermal breakdown
rules.physicsNote: High RPM increases oil shear rate exponentially. Polymer viscosity improvers in multi-grade oils break down under sustained shear, permanently reducing viscosity.

rules.name: Prolonged Heat Stress on Oil
rules.description: Engine has spent significant time in heat stress conditions — oil degradation accelerated
possibleCauses: Frequent towing or heavy load driving
rules.physicsNote: Oil oxidation rate doubles every 10°C above 100°C (Arrhenius equation). Heat stress fraction > 25% means the oil has been continuously stressed — TBN depletion accelerates.

transmission.description: Monitors automatic/CVT transmission health using TCM-specific parameters (Mode 22 DIDs via UDS/ISO-TP, requires Rust agent). Cannot be read via standard Chrome GATT OBD Mode 01.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Overheating
rules.description: Transmission fluid temperature at critical overheating level (≥120°C)
rules.physicsNote: ATF oxidation doubles for every 10°C above 90°C. At 120°C, ATF life is reduced to ~50 hours. Clutch friction material degrades rapidly.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid High Temperature
rules.description: Transmission fluid in warning zone (100-120°C)
possibleCauses: Heavy towing or sustained gradient climb
rules.physicsNote: This range is recoverable but indicates cooling system marginal for current duty cycle. Long-term operation here degrades fluid faster than service intervals account for.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Cold
rules.description: Transmission fluid below 10°C — cold shift quality affected, risk of varnish if operated hard
rules.physicsNote: ATF viscosity increases significantly below 10°C, causing engagement lag and pressure buildup issues. Designed for Kota Kinabalu highland use or air-conditioned parking structures.

rules.name: Limp Mode Active
rules.description: TCM has commanded limp home mode — severe internal fault detected
rules.triggerConditions: TCM limp mode DTC flag SET in TCM DTC data
rules.physicsNote: Limp mode restricts transmission to typically 2nd or 3rd gear at limited shift points. This is a protective strategy to allow vehicle movement while preventing further damage from uncontrolled shifting.

rules.name: High Torque Converter Slip
rules.description: Torque converter slip exceeding 15% at cruise — TC clutch failing to lock
rules.triggerConditions: TC Slip = (Input speed - Output speed) / Input speed > 15%
possibleCauses: Hydraulic pressure insufficient for TC lockup
rules.physicsNote: TC slip above 5% at highway cruise wastes significant fuel (TCC is designed to lock at cruise for ~3-5% fuel economy improvement). Above 15% generates substantial heat.

rules.name: Torque Converter Lockup Failure
rules.description: TC clutch unable to lock despite conditions exceeding lockup threshold (speed > 80 km/h, slip > 5%)
rules.triggerConditions: Speed > 80 km/h AND TC Slip > 5% sustained
possibleCauses: TC clutch plate worn beyond engagement
rules.physicsNote: At highway speed the torque converter should be fully locked (0% slip) for efficiency. Sustained slip at speed generates heat proportional to torque × slip speed — leading to fluid degradation.

rules.name: Gear Ratio Deviation
rules.description: Actual gear ratio deviating > 10% from nominal — clutch pack or band slipping
possibleCauses: Clutch pack wear in current gear
rules.physicsNote: Gear ratio is theoretically fixed by planetary gear tooth counts. Deviation indicates slippage in the clutch or band holding that gear — mechanical engagement quality issue.

rules.name: CVT Belt/Chain Slip
rules.description: CVT variator ratio fluctuating > 5% at steady state — belt/chain slipping on pulley
rules.triggerConditions: CVT ratio variance > 5% fluctuation at steady throttle/speed
possibleCauses: Clutch pack gripping the variator inconsistently
rules.physicsNote: CVT belts/chains transmit torque by clamping force on conical pulleys. Slip indicates insufficient clamping hydraulic pressure or belt wear. Progressive slip rapidly destroys belt and pulley running surfaces.

rules.name: Transmission Overheating While Driving
rules.description: Trans fluid above 120°C while driving — active overheating condition
rules.physicsNote: Overheating while driving is worse than at idle — continued load generation keeps adding heat. Must stop and cool down.

rules.name: Transmission Not Warming Up
rules.description: Trans fluid still cold after 10+ minutes of driving — cooler bypass issue
rules.triggerConditions: Trans temp < 40°C AND runtime > 600s AND speed > 40 km/h
rules.physicsNote: Cold ATF viscosity is 10-100x higher than operating temp. This causes harsh shifts, increased pump effort, and accelerated wear of friction materials.

rules.name: Transmission Temperature Spike
rules.description: Trans fluid above 130°C — extreme thermal event, clutch material glazing
rules.physicsNote: Above 130°C, ATF breaks down rapidly and clutch friction material begins to glaze. Glazed clutches cannot grip — creating a self-reinforcing cycle of slip and heat.

rules.name: RPM/Speed Ratio Abnormal
rules.description: Engine RPM disproportionately high for vehicle speed — possible slippage
rules.triggerConditions: RPM > 4000 AND speed > 50 km/h (ratio abnormally high)
rules.physicsNote: At 50+ km/h, most transmissions should be in a higher gear with RPM below 3000. RPM > 4000 at this speed means significant power loss through slippage.

rules.name: Engine Lugging Detected
rules.description: High engine load at low RPM while moving — wrong gear for conditions
rules.triggerConditions: Load > 70% AND RPM < 1500 AND speed > 30 km/h
possibleCauses: Too high a gear for grade/load
rules.physicsNote: Lugging keeps combustion pressure high with insufficient airflow for cooling. Promotes carbon buildup and knock. Modern ECUs retard timing to protect but at a significant power and efficiency cost.

rules.name: Trans Fluid Critical Temperature
rules.description: Trans fluid above 135°C — seal damage and fluid carbonization imminent
rules.physicsNote: ATF degrades exponentially above 120°C. At 135°C, fluid life halves every 10 minutes. Seals harden, friction material glazes, and varnish forms on valve body.

rules.name: Trans Hot During Coasting
rules.description: Trans temp elevated despite low load (coasting) — internal friction or cooler bypass fault
rules.triggerConditions: Trans temp > 100°C AND load < 15% AND speed > 50 km/h
rules.physicsNote: During coast, trans load is minimal — temp should stabilize or decrease. Elevated temp under no-load means internal friction is generating heat when it shouldn't.

rules.name: RPM Flare During Shift
rules.description: RPM spikes during what should be a smooth shift — clutch engagement delayed
rules.triggerConditions: RPM spike > 500 RPM above baseline AND speed steady
rules.physicsNote: During a normal shift, RPM drops as new gear engages within 300ms. A 500+ RPM flare means the off-going clutch released before the on-coming clutch engaged — the gearbox is momentarily in neutral.

rules.name: Speed/RPM Ratio Unstable at Cruise
rules.description: RPM/speed ratio fluctuating at steady cruise — gear hunting or TC lockup cycling
rules.triggerConditions: RPM/speed ratio std dev > 5 AND speed between 50-90 km/h AND throttle < 20%
possibleCauses: Throttle position signal noise to TCM
rules.physicsNote: At cruise, the drivetrain ratio should be rock-steady. Fluctuation means the trans is repeatedly selecting/deselecting gears or TC lockup — causes increased wear and poor fuel economy.

rules.name: Trans Heat Soak at Extended Idle
rules.description: Trans temp rising during extended idle in gear — TC converting engine torque to heat
rules.triggerConditions: Trans temp > 95°C AND speed < 5 km/h AND runtime > 600s
possibleCauses: High idle RPM increasing TC heat
Trans cooler flow insufficient at idle
rules.physicsNote: In Drive at idle, the TC absorbs engine torque as heat. At 700 RPM, this is ~1-2 kW of heat. Without vehicle motion, ram air is zero and the cooler relies on fan alone.

rules.name: Wrong Gear for Speed/Load
rules.description: Vehicle in a gear inappropriate for the speed and load combination — potential solenoid or valve body issue
rules.triggerConditions: Load > 50% AND RPM < 1200 AND speed > 40 km/h
possibleCauses: Shift solenoid stuck in high gear
rules.physicsNote: High load at low RPM means the engine is lugging because the trans won't downshift. Normally, the TCM commands a downshift when load exceeds 40-50% at low RPM.

rules.name: Trans Temp Not Cooling at Highway Speed
rules.description: Trans temp not decreasing at highway speed — cooler circuit or flow fault
rules.triggerConditions: Trans temp > 105°C AND speed > 80 km/h AND load < 40%
possibleCauses: Trans cooler line kinked or blocked
rules.physicsNote: At highway speed with moderate load, trans cooler receives maximum airflow. Temp should be 80-95°C. Above 105°C despite good airflow means heat rejection capacity is compromised.
